Who Won the Week: Alan Bergman

He may be an extreme long-shot horse in the C.E.O. succession race, but the=
 Disney film chief=E2=80=99s record $389 million global debut for Moana 2, =
first developed as a series for Disney+, might be the greatest pivot/salvag=
e in movie history.

Shot: Let=E2=80=99s put this wild weekend at the box office in terms Netfli=
x co-C.E.O.s Ted Sarandos and Greg Peters can understand. Nearly 30 million=
 people saw one of either Moana 2, Wicked, or Gladiator II in the U.S. and =
Canada this weekend, per Screendollars ( https://live.screendollars.com/wp-=
content/uploads/2024/12/SCREENDOLLARS-NEWSLETTER-2024-12-1.pdf?utm_campaign=
=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F2%2F24%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%=
27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F2%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_s=
ource=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ). Multiply that by the a=
verage running time of the films (136 minutes), and it=E2=80=99s 4.1 billio=
n minutes watched. Compare that to the top three English-language films on =
Netflix for the full week of November 18-24 globally=E2=80=94The Merry Gent=
leman, Hot Frosty, and Spellbound. Together, they delivered 3.39 billion mi=
nutes watched, so an average of 1.1 billion minutes, which is about one-qua=
rter the domestic in-theater time over five
days. And the Netflix stat is worldwide.

The economics are different, of course. Wicked, Gladiator II, and Moana 2 t=
ogether cost at least $550 million to produce and hundreds of millions more=
 to market, far more than the two quickie Christmas movies and a Skydance a=
nimated pic on Netflix, which markets its films
far less. Netflix movies are also co-viewed by many at home. But domestic a=
udiences alone paid a collective $389 million to see the theatrical movies =
over the five days, and each will have a long life on their respective stre=
amers. Something to remember next time Sarandos says Netflix delivers the l=
argest possible audience for movies.

Chaser: But=E2=80=A6 even after this record Thanksgiving, the 2024 domestic=
 box office is still running 11 percent behind last year and 27 percent off=
 the 2019 total at this point. Survive to =E2=80=9925 remains the goal.

Runner-up: Ron Meyer and Stacey Snider, the former Universal execs, who fir=
st acquired film rights to the Wicked novel for producer Marc Platt back in=
 the =E2=80=9990s, before the stage musical existed. That deal led to Unive=
rsal (with a $10 million investment) and Platt co-producing the Broadway sh=
ow, along with David Stone, and quietly reaping a hefty share of
the $5 billion in tickets sold worldwide. And now, more than 25 years later=
, a movie blockbuster=E2=80=94which, of course, is also juicing grosses for=
 the stage play.

, including Disney=E2=80=99s resurgence, the reframed success of =E2=80=98G=
ladiator II,=E2=80=99 and a new weekend calibration for =E2=80=98Wicked: Pa=
rt One.=E2=80=99

Theaters finally have something to be thankful for: This weekend=E2=80=99s =
lineup set a new Thanksgiving record with around $427 million in North Amer=
ica for the five days. It was mostly driven by two family movies, Moana 2 a=
nd Wicked: Part One, which pulled in best-case-scenario box office despite =
opening almost concurrently. Disney=E2=80=99s Bob Iger and Alan Bergman cle=
arly made the right call with Moana 2, which was originally a streaming ser=
ies before Disney opted for a theatrical sequel that hauled over $225 milli=
on from Wednesday to Sunday, nearly doubling Frozen II=E2=80=99s $125 milli=
on take over the long weekend in 2019 (it had opened the previous Friday).

Likewise, Donna Langley=E2=80=99s decision to move Universal=E2=80=99s bewi=
tching musical up to the pre-Thanksgiving weekend to position it as the sea=
son=E2=80=99s
YA fantasy tentpole paid off. Wicked: Part One continued to play like Harry=
 Potter and the Twilight of the Hunger Games, notching an $81 million secon=
d weekend and $118 million from Wednesday to Sunday. That was down just 28 =
percent from its $112.5 million opening, the smallest second-weekend drop-o=
ff ever for a $100 million-plus opener.

In the end, two kid-friendly, female-centric musical empowerment fantasies =
earned a combined $344 million over the holiday. It wasn=E2=80=99t a Barben=
heimer-style cultural event, which makes the numbers arguably at least as i=
mpressive. Moreover, the double-barreled success illuminates several realit=
ies about the current box office environment.

1. There=E2=80=99s Room for Two

Hollywood should no longer fear scheduling multiple big films near one anot=
her=E2=80=94as long as their demographic bases are complementary but don=E2=
=80=99t entirely overlap. Moana 2 and Wicked shared elements and themes tha=
t defied box office gravity merely through respective consumer interest. Ju=
st as the likes of World War Z could thrive alongside Monsters University i=
n previous years, there was even room for Paramount=E2=80=99s Gladiator II=
=E2=80=94also in its second weekend=E2=80=94to earn another $44 million. In=
 fact, the R-rated action-drama has already earned $111 million domesticall=
y and $320 million worldwide. (It opened internationally two weeks ago.) If=
 audiences want to see a film, or two, or three, they=E2=80=99ll find the t=
ime.

2. Theaters Are Revenue Drivers

Since the end of the pandemic, the controlling narrative of the theatrical =
business has been its inability to return to pre-Covid levels of box office=
, itself the result of many factors=E2=80=94fewer productions, sending movi=
es to streaming, the strikes, etcetera. But the yearly domestic and interna=
tional cumes obscure the fact that major theatrical releases regularly perf=
orm at or above pre-Covid levels. Since early 2021, when Godzilla vs. Kong =
and A Quiet Place Part II played about as well as would have been expected =
in non-Covid circumstances, we=E2=80=99ve seen new box office opening weeke=
nd records for almost every major holiday=E2=80=94Easter, Memorial Day, Ind=
ependence Day, Labor Day, and now Thanksgiving. In fact, seven of the top 2=
0 all-time domestic and global grossers have been released since Spider-Man=
: No Way Home premiered
almost three years ago.

The theatrical potential of the franchise-friendly tentpole is as strong as=
 ever, and there=E2=80=99s also still room for smaller breakouts like Free =
Guy, Everything Everywhere All at Once, It Ends With Us, and Longlegs. Yes,=
 the industry slate is still skewed toward all-quadrant, family-friendly fr=
anchise films that can justify an afternoon out with the kids, and there=E2=
=80=99s still the matter of retraining audiences to come out for mainstream=
 awards contenders like Conclave (a relative success at $31 million domesti=
c, but it would have arguably earned far more in a less event-film-focused =
ecosystem).

But Hollywood got the message when it comes to franchises and I.P.: That=E2=
=80=99s why Margot Robbie accepted less upfront cash so Wuthering Heights c=
ould become a Warner Bros. theatrical release rather than a Netflix
Original. And that=E2=80=99s why Iger and Bergman decided not to forgo box =
office revenue for Moana 2, which will still pull good-to-incredible number=
s on Disney+. During Covid, executives like WarnerMedia=E2=80=99s Jason Kil=
ar convinced the industry that streaming versus theatrical was a binary cho=
ice. In reality, it never was, and the zero-sum mentality did harm to both =
ecosystems.

3. The 10-Day Opening Weekend

We should measure the strength of a pre-Thanksgiving opener not just by the=
 Friday-Sunday gross but by the overall 10-day haul, which allows for post-=
release factors like word of mouth. Folks who wanted to see Gladiator II an=
d Wicked: Part One but lacked the time for both last weekend made at least =
some effort to catch up over the holiday. Ditto those who only wanted to se=
e one but didn=E2=80=99t mind waiting for the holiday weekend=E2=80=94or th=
e Monday to Wednesday corridor, which many kids now have off from school. W=
e=E2=80=99ve seen this extended frame phenomenon play out in recent years w=
ith Frozen II (a $130 million opening weekend and then a $125 million Wedne=
sday-Sunday) and The Hunger Games: The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes (a $4=
5 million debut, followed by $42 million during the long holiday weekend).

4. A Good-for-Theaters-Bad-for-Studios Box Office Success

Gladiator II has earned $320 million worldwide so far, with a conventional =
rate of descent suggesting a $175 million domestic and $440 million worldwi=
de finish. I=E2=80=99d expect longer legs and a possible $500 million-plus =
finish due to decent word of mouth (a 4/5 on PostTrak was apparently more r=
epresentative of consumer buzz than the B CinemaScore) and the near-total l=
ack of tentpole-sized, adult-skewing and/or non-fantastical action movies d=
ue out between now and Captain America: Brave New World on Valentine=E2=80=
=99s Day. But with Gladiator II=E2=80=99s $250 million budget, even the bes=
t-case scenario might not be good enough.

However, the strong reviews, solid buzz, and solid box office numbers affir=
m that moviegoers are buying what 87-year-old Ridley Scott is selling. If G=
ladiator II doesn=E2=80=99t qualify as a
rate-of-return theatrical success, it should still qualify as a bad-for-the=
-studio-but-good-for-theaters situation. We saw that last year as well when=
 pricey Disney flops like Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny still put m=
ore butts in seats than some of their rivals=E2=80=99 box office successes.=
 Multiplexes will take The Little Mermaid ($570 million) over Five Nights a=
t Freddy=E2=80=99s ($291 million) all day long, even if Mermaid didn=E2=80=
=99t pencil out for Disney because it cost so much.

If theatrical success is now valued mainly for its ability to extend and in=
crease a film=E2=80=99s long-term earning potential across platforms and ec=
osystems, Gladiator II should be a highlight on Paramount=E2=80=99s P&L for=
 years to come, even if it doesn=E2=80=99t break even via theatrical revenu=
e alone.

5. Disney Is Back-ish

With the largest global animated opening of all time at $389 million, it=E2=
=80=99s pretty likely that Moana 2 will become Disney=E2=80=99s third $1 bi=
llion-plus earner of 2024, alongside Inside Out 2 and Deadpool & Wolverine.=
 Even if Mufasa: The Lion King doesn=E2=80=99t follow suit next month=E2=80=
=94and, yes, Alice Through the Looking Glass and Maleficent: Mistress of Ev=
il both earned noticeably less than their predecessors=E2=80=94Disney has r=
eclaimed its tentpole dominance.

But the company is relying heavily on 2010s nostalgia. Audiences showed up =
for new installments of Disney=E2=80=99s most popular films from the past d=
ecade (as well as follow-ups to previously Fox franchises like Deadpool, Al=
ien, and Planet of the Apes), but it=E2=80=99s been 20 years since Disney=
=E2=80=99s last successful new-to-cinema live-action
franchise, National Treasure. (Though if Wild Hogs 2 does gangbusters, I gu=
ess it=E2=80=99ll only have been 18 or 19 years.) Moreover, Pixar=E2=80=99s=
 Coco=E2=80=94released over Thanksgiving 2017=E2=80=94remains Hollywood=E2=
=80=99s last non-sequel, not-based-on-previous-I.P. animated film to top $5=
00 million worldwide.

I cannot overstate the extent to which Elio, the original Pixar sci-fi fant=
asy set to open June 13, is Disney=E2=80=99s most important movie next year=
=2E The current Moana 2/Wicked: Part=
 One matchup implies that, if it=E2=80=99=
s any good, Elio could suffer minimal damage from opening on the same day a=
s Universal=E2=80=99s How to Train Your Dragon live-action remake. Elementa=
l earned $342 million overseas, which was on par with the $220 million=E2=
=80=93$370 million likes of Cars, Wall-E, Brave, Monsters Inc., and The Inc=
redibles. It was the domestic gross=E2=80=94just $154 million=E2=80=94that =
fell short of the customary $200
million=E2=80=93$240 million North American totals, inflation notwithstandi=
ng, for the average Pixar non-sequel.

If Elio can hold firm overseas and play like one of those prior classics in=
 North America, then Disney will at least have a Pixar original that perfor=
ms like a Pixar original. Remember too that Coco only topped $800 million b=
ecause of its sky-high=E2=80=94and now highly unlikely=E2=80=94$189 million=
 from China. Without that boost, the Thanksgiving 2017 release would have e=
nded up in the $530 million=E2=80=93$660 million ballpark alongside success=
ful Disney toons like Big Hero 6, Ralph Breaks the Internet, Tangled, and M=
oana.

Perhaps Covid-era movies like Encanto and Soul=E2=80=94along with possibly =
Luca, Raya and the Last Dragon, and Turning Red=E2=80=94would have been box=
 office triples or outright home runs in other times. Perhaps Disney never =
really stopped making all-quadrant, globally appealing
original animated films but rather, temporarily undercut the distribution m=
ethod that made them possible. And with a firm 60-day window and presumed g=
oodwill from Inside Out 2 and Moana 2, maybe Disney will discover, to parap=
hrase a line from that other Thanksgiving franchise, that it had the brains=
, heart, and courage to successfully release blockbuster animated originals=
 all along.

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Theaters finally ha=
ve something to be thankful for: This weekend=E2=80=99s lineup set a new Th=
anksgiving record with around $427 million in North America for the five da=
ys. It was mostly driven by two family movies, <i>Moana 2</i> and <i>Wicked=
: Part One</i>, which pulled in best-case-scenario box office despite openi=
ng almost concurrently. Disney=E2=80=99s <b>Bob Iger </b>and<b> Alan Bergma=
n </b>clearly made the right call with <i>Moana 2</i>, which was originally=
 a streaming series before Disney opted for a theatrical sequel that hauled=
 over $225 million from Wednesday to Sunday, nearly doubling <i>Frozen II</=
i>=E2=80=99s $125 million take over the long weekend in 2019 (it had opened=
 the previous Friday). <br/><br/>Likewise, <b>Donna Langley</b>=E2=80=99s d=
ecision to move Universal=E2=80=99s bewitching musical up to the pre-Thanks=
giving weekend to position it as the season=E2=80=99s
YA fantasy tentpole paid off. <i>Wicked: Part One </i>continued to play lik=
e <i>Harry Potter and the Twilight of the Hunger Games</i>, notching an $81=
 million second weekend and $118 million from Wednesday to Sunday. That was=
 down just 28 percent from its $112.5 million opening, the smallest second-=
weekend drop-off ever for a $100 million-plus opener. <br/><br/>In the end,=
 two kid-friendly, female-centric musical empowerment fantasies earned a co=
mbined $344 million over the holiday. It wasn=E2=80=99t a Barbenheimer-styl=
e cultural event, which makes the numbers arguably at least as impressive. =
Moreover, the double-barreled success illuminates several realities about t=
he current box office environment.<br/></td>
         </tr>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Hollywood should no=
 longer fear scheduling multiple big films near one another=E2=80=94as long=
 as their demographic bases are complementary but don=E2=80=99t <i>entirely=
</i> overlap. <i>Moana 2 </i>and <i>Wicked</i> shared elements and themes t=
hat defied box office gravity merely through respective consumer interest. =
Just as the likes of <i>World War Z </i>could thrive alongside <i>Monsters =
University </i>in previous years, there was even room for Paramount=E2=80=
=99s <i>Gladiator II</i>=E2=80=94also in its second weekend=E2=80=94to earn=
 another $44 million. In fact, the R-rated action-drama has already earned =
$111 million domestically and $320 million worldwide. (It opened internatio=
nally two weeks ago.) If audiences want to see a film, or two, or three, th=
ey=E2=80=99ll find the time.</td>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">The theatrical pote=
ntial of the franchise-friendly tentpole is as strong as ever, and there=E2=
=80=99s also still room for smaller breakouts like <i>Free Guy, Everything =
Everywhere All at Once, It Ends With Us</i>, and <i>Longlegs</i>. Yes, the =
industry slate is still skewed toward all-quadrant, family-friendly franchi=
se films that can justify an afternoon out with the kids, and there=E2=80=
=99s still the matter of retraining audiences to come out for mainstream aw=
ards contenders like <i>Conclave </i>(a relative success at $31 million dom=
estic, but it would have arguably earned far more in a less event-film-focu=
sed ecosystem). <br/><br/>But Hollywood got the message when it comes to fr=
anchises and I.P.: That=E2=80=99s why <b>Margot Robbie </b>accepted less up=
front cash so <i>Wuthering Heights </i>could become a Warner Bros. theatric=
al release rather than a Netflix
Original. And that=E2=80=99s why Iger and Bergman decided not to forgo box =
office revenue for <i>Moana 2</i>, which will still pull good-to-incredible=
 numbers on Disney+. During Covid, executives like WarnerMedia=E2=80=99s <b=
>Jason Kilar </b>convinced the industry that streaming versus theatrical wa=
s a binary choice. In reality, it never was, and the zero-sum mentality did=
 harm to both ecosystems.<br/></td>

 Part One </i>but lacked the time for both last weekend made at least some =
effort to catch up over the holiday. Ditto those who only wanted to see one=
 but didn=E2=80=99t mind waiting for the holiday weekend=E2=80=94or the Mon=
day to Wednesday corridor, which many kids now have off from school. We=E2=
=80=99ve seen this extended frame phenomenon play out in recent years with =

 </i>(a $45 million debut, followed by $42 million during the long holiday =

=E2=80=99d expect longer legs and a possible $500 million-plus finish due t=

/i>doesn=E2=80=99t qualify as a

extend and increase a film=E2=80=99s long-term earning potential across pla=
tforms and ecosystems, <i>Gladiator II </i>should be a highlight on Paramou=

en via theatrical revenue alone.<br/></td>

remains Hollywood=E2=80=99s last non-sequel, not-based-on-previous-I.P. ani=
mated film to top $500 million worldwide.<br/><br/>I cannot overstate the e=
xtent to which <i>Elio</i>, the original Pixar sci-fi fantasy set to open J=
une 13, is Disney=E2=80=99s most important movie next year. The current <i>=

ny good, <i>Elio</i> could suffer minimal damage from opening on the same d=

e. <i>Elemental </i>earned $342 million overseas, which was on par with the=
 $220 million=E2=80=93$370 million likes of <i>Cars, Wall-E</i>,<i> Brave</=
i>,<i> Monsters Inc.</i>, and <i>The Incredibles. </i>It was the domestic g=
ross=E2=80=94just $154 million=E2=80=94that fell short of the customary $20=
0

ng, for the average Pixar non-sequel. <br/><br/>If <i>Elio </i>can hold fir=

 Disney will at least have a Pixar original that performs like a Pixar orig=

 sky-high=E2=80=94and now highly unlikely=E2=80=94$189 million from China. =

ons like <i>Big Hero 6,</i> <i>Ralph Breaks the Internet</i>, <i>Tangled</i=
>, and <i>Moana</i>. <br/><br/>Perhaps Covid-era movies like <i>Encanto </i=
